summary: Jeff Atwood explores programming-specific fonts after reading Mike Gunderloy's suggestion to customize IDE fonts beyond the default Courier New. He compiles a visual comparison of popular monospace fonts including Andale Mono, Anonymous, Lucida Console, Monaco, Pragmata, ProFont, and Proggy Clean. Through testing, he concludes that monospace is essential, anti-aliasing is undesirable for code, and bitmap fonts offer precision but lack scalability. His personal recommendation is Proggy for most situations, with Lucida Typewriter or Pragmata as scalable alternatives for high-resolution displays.
key_points: Monospace fonts are essential for programming - proportional fonts don't work in an IDE
Anti-aliasing (including ClearType) is undesirable for programming fonts at small sizes
Bitmap fonts like Proggy offer superior precision but cannot scale, which matters on high-resolution displays
Font choice should adapt to your display resolution - what works at 1600x1200 may not work at 1920x1440
Some fonts auto-apply anti-aliasing even at 9 point, making them unsuitable for code
money_quotes: Lists of fonts are all well and good, but a picture is worth a thousand words.
I don't care for anti-aliasing of any kind on a programming font.
Bitmap fonts, such as Proggy, are very precise but don't scale. At all.
I don't recommend using Comic Sans as your programming font.
Note that some fonts decide to anti-alias themselves even at 9 point!
key_insight: The right programming font is a surprisingly personal choice that depends on your display resolution, anti-aliasing preferences, and whether you value precision over scalability.
